data.title: Gilead Sciences snaps six straight sessions of losses
data.description:
Gilead Sciences (GILD) shares snapped a six-session losing streak, closing 0.56% higher at $133.64 on Thursday.

The biopharmaceutical company began its downward trend on April 15, and the stock lost nearly 5% over the preceding six sessions.

GILD closed 0.3% lower at $132.90 on Wednesday. The firm’s shares have slipped more than 2% over the past month.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, GILD has a Hold rating with a score of 3.11 out of 5. The company received an A+ for profitability prospects, while it got a D+ for revisions.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 22 out of 30 analysts rated GILD a Buy or higher, while eight gave it a Hold recommendation, and none recommended Sell or lower.

However, Seeking Alpha analysts are cautious and view the stock as a Hold.

Seeking Alpha analyst Edmund Ingham highlighted that despite a recent $11B M&A spree targeting oncology and immunology, GILD faces skepticism due to a poor track record and a lack of near-term revenue contributions from new acquisitions.

“I expect Q1 earnings to be somewhat underwhelming, with sluggish growth and minor IPR&D charges, but GILD trades at a below-average forward P/E of ~16x versus peers,” Ingham noted.

He added that I continued to hold GILD for its robust HIV franchise and a dividend yield of over 2%, while awaiting evidence that management can successfully execute on oncology ambitions.

The stock rose 8.6% last year, compared to a 4.3% gain in the broader S&P 500 Index.

data.title: T-Mobile raises share repurchases by $3.2B
data.description:
• T-Mobile’s (TMUS) board has approved a $3.6B increase to its share repurchase agreement, bringing the total number of shares to be purchased to $18.2B.
  • The share repurchase agreement will run until the end of 2026.
  • The shares will be purchased with cash on hand and the proceeds of one or more debt issuances.
  • Source: SEC filing

data.title: Bread Financial outlines 2026 loan growth up low single digits while targeting 7.2%-7.4% net loss rate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bread Financial (BFH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Today, Bread Financial reported strong first quarter results, which were underscored by a return to loan growth alongside increasing growth in credit sales and continued improvement in our credit metrics.” (President, CEO & Director Ralph Andretta)
  • “On the new brand partner front, we were excited to launch new credit card relationships with Ford and Ethan Allen in the quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Andretta)
  • “We are also offering Bread Pay installment loans for AAA, Dell and Ford as we continue to expand this product offering.” (President, CEO & Director Andretta)
  • “Our first quarter financial results highlight our company's strong capital and cash flow generation, earning net income of $181 million, generating revenue growth of 5% year-over-year and growing tangible book value per common share by 26% to $61.57.” (President, CEO & Director Andretta)
  • “During the quarter, credit sales of $6.5 billion increased 7% year-over-year.” (Executive VP & CFO Perry Beberman)

Outlook

  • “Our 2026 outlook is unchanged.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “We expect full year 2026 average credit card and other loans -- the loan growth to be up low single digits compared to 2025.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “Total revenue growth is anticipated to be up low single digits, largely in line with average loan growth.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “We expect second quarter total expenses to be up sequentially from the first quarter… Initial estimates of the second quarter expenses are just under $500 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “Specifically for the second quarter, we expect this dynamic to pressure noninterest income up to $40 million compared to the first quarter of 2026.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “Given the ongoing gradual improvement in our credit metrics, we are on track to achieve a net loss rate at the low end of our 7.2% to 7.4% targeted range for 2026.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)

Financial Results

  • “We are pleased that loan growth has inflected positively as average loans increased 1% to $18.3 billion and end-of-period loans increased 2% to $18.1 billion.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “Revenue increased $48 million, or 5%, primarily reflecting the implementation of pricing changes and lower interest expense, partially offset by lower billed late fees and higher retailer share arrangements.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “In total, we generated net income of $181 million and diluted EPS of $4.15.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “Net interest margin of 19.3% increased year-over-year and sequentially as loan yields continue to benefit from the gradual build of pricing changes and funding costs continue to improve.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “Our delinquency rate for the first quarter was 5.59%, down 34 basis points from last year and down 16 basis points sequentially.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)

Q&A

  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: “I'm a bit surprised to see loan growth and revenue growth guidance kind of low single digits.” Executive VP & CFO Beberman: “With the degree of uncertainty in the macro environment, it feels a little premature to declare a victory yet that we can then up it.”
  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: “how much of future share repurchases are based off of having to raise those preferred equities?” Executive VP & CFO Beberman: “The cadence and the amount that we're able to use this year will somewhat be dependent on, obviously, earnings generation and the preferred share issuance.”
  • Natalie Howe, BofA Securities: “what else are you looking at for the year as levers for NIM stability?” Executive VP & CFO Beberman: “I'd like to say it's going to be reasonably stable… but there's a lot of moving parts.”
  • Natalie Howe, BofA Securities: “how durable is that as a driver right now?” President, CEO & Director Andretta: “T&E has been a strong category for us for some time, and we see it being a strong category in the future.”
  • William Ryan, Seaport: “how much impact is that going to have on the receivables growth going forward?” Executive VP & CFO Beberman: “So there is no effect in total.”
  • William Ryan, Seaport: “the NFL portfolio… what those changes were?” President, CEO & Director Andretta: “We're still the issuer of the NFL card.”
  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: “are there specific verticals… for potential… new partnerships?” President, CEO & Director Andretta: “The pipeline continues to be robust.”
  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: “how are you thinking about that… for both credit and spend?” Executive VP & CFO Beberman: “We're cautious… But the consumer right now is resilient.”
  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: “late fee mitigation impacts… how does it sequence over the course of the year?” Executive VP & CFO Beberman: “you're going to see a… gradually declining amount of benefit.”
  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: “Is the target the same or a little bit lower… [for] the charge-off rate?” Executive VP & CFO Beberman: “we're still looking to get to a loss rate target that is around 6% or below.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • “I'm a bit surprised” and repeated macro questions signaled slightly negative analyst tone, while questions on buybacks, NIM stability, and late-fee dynamics pressed for durability and cadence. (Vincent Caintic, Natalie Howe, Sanjay Sakhrani)
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks (“strong first quarter results”) but more cautious in Q&A (“it feels a little premature to declare a victory yet” and “We're cautious”). (Executive VP & CFO Beberman; President, CEO & Director Andretta)
  • Versus last quarter, management language moved from “we are nearing an inflection point for loan growth as we enter 2026” to “a return to loan growth,” while macro uncertainty remained a repeated qualifier. (President, CEO & Director Andretta; Executive VP & CFO Beberman)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Loan growth framing shifted from “we are nearing an inflection point for loan growth as we enter 2026” to “a return to loan growth.” (President, CEO & Director Andretta)
  • Capital return messaging expanded from “$240 million remaining on our current share repurchase authorization” to “our remaining stock repurchase authorization was $690 million,” alongside a new emphasis that “we look to further optimize our capital structure in the future by issuing additional preferred shares.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • Prior-quarter NIM commentary highlighted Fed cuts as a headwind (“lower… Fed and prime rates will pressure NIM”), while this quarter emphasized funding and repricing progress (“most of the portfolio is repriced”). (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Consumers are being thoughtful and budgeting actively amid lower sentiment and confidence and higher fuel costs.” (President, CEO & Director Andretta)
  • “We continue to apply prudent weightings on the economic scenarios… including ongoing uncertainty regarding trade policy and global conflicts and then the downstream impacts on inflation and unemployment.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)
  • “Specifically for the second quarter, we expect this dynamic to pressure noninterest income up to $40 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Beberman)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as “strong” with “a return to loan growth,” credit sales growth, and improving credit metrics, while reaffirming a full-year outlook calling for low-single-digit loan and revenue growth and a 2026 net loss rate at “the low end” of the 7.2% to 7.4% range. Executives emphasized new partner launches (including Ford and Ethan Allen), ongoing pricing-change benefits that “slow throughout the year,” and a more cautious stance on the macro backdrop, including fuel-price sensitivity and uncertainty tied to trade policy and conflicts, while also signaling that buyback cadence will depend on capital generation and potential preferred issuance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: EastGroup projects 2026 FFO of $9.52 per share while increasing development starts guidance to $265M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: EastGroup Properties (EGP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Marshall Loeb framed the quarter around portfolio resilience, saying, "Our first quarter results demonstrate our portfolio quality and resiliency within the industrial market." He highlighted "funds from operations omitting involuntary conversions of $2.30 per share" and noted, "Quarter end leasing was 96.5% with occupancy at 95.9%."

  • Loeb emphasized leasing economics and tenant diversification, stating, "Quarterly re-leasing spreads were 37% GAAP and 20% cash for leases signed during the quarter" and adding, "our top 10 tenants falling to 6.7% of rents." He also pointed to a key operational split: "the portfolio is well leased, while development leasing has been taking a little longer."

  • President R. Dunbar described a measured-but-improving development leasing backdrop, saying, "businesses continue to operate amid headline volatility and decision cycles continue to remain extended." He raised full-year development starts guidance, stating, "we are increasing our guidance for the year to $265 million," and added, "we commenced construction on 4 projects totaling 586,000 square feet, of which 27% is pre-leased."

  • CFO Staci Tyler attributed Q1 upside and reiterated balance sheet positioning: "FFO exceeded the midpoint of our guidance range at $2.30 per share, excluding gains on involuntary conversion" and "The outperformance in first quarter was primarily driven by lower-than-anticipated G&A expense and higher-than-projected property net operating income." She also cited a credit catalyst: "Moody's ratings upgraded our issuer rating to Baa1 with a stable outlook."

  • Loeb flagged leadership changes, saying, "Our executive team restructuring is nicely falling into place. I'm excited to welcome Jim Traynor to the team" and "John Coleman... is entering a well-earned retirement on June 30."

Outlook

  • Tyler guided Q2 FFO, stating, "FFO for second quarter is estimated to be in the range of $2.30 to $2.38 per share," and raised the annual outlook: "we increased the midpoint of our 2026 FFO guidance to $9.52 per share, excluding gain from involuntary conversion."

  • She increased same-property and occupancy assumptions, saying, "we raised the midpoint of our guidance assumption by 10 basis points to 6.2%" for cash same-property NOI and guided to "expected same-property occupancy of 96.4%."

  • Compared with last quarter’s guide, management moved development starts higher from $250 million to $265 million (Dunbar: "we are increasing our guidance for the year to $265 million"), while Tyler described the leasing mix shift inside development NOI: "most of that $0.03... has moved from speculative leasing to signed leases" and "the remaining $0.04 is speculative leasing."

Financial Results

  • Loeb reported operating strength and spreads: "Quarter end leasing was 96.5% with occupancy at 95.9%" and "Quarterly cash same-store NOI rose a strong 9.2%."

  • Tyler detailed balance sheet capacity and leverage metrics: "We ended the quarter with no balance drawn on our unsecured bank credit facility, leaving available capacity of $675 million," with "debt to total market capitalization of 14%" and "interest and fixed charge coverage of 14.8x."

  • She also disclosed capital markets actions affecting funding mix, stating, "We issued $70 million in common stock through our common equity offering program at over $191 per share" and "an additional $50 million in forward equity sale agreements available for issuance at over $196 per share."

Q&A

  • Craig Mailman, Citigroup: asked whether tenants are moving faster and about remaining under-leased deliveries; President Dunbar said, "we are actually seeing some tenants move a little bit quicker," citing Atlanta competition and added, "we've got about 775,000 square feet" of first-gen space.

  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo: asked how much spec development leasing is embedded in guidance and whether it’s risk/upside; CFO Tyler said, "we have about $0.04 of NOI for speculative development leasing in the second half of the year" and added, "We see that more as an opportunity."

  • Samir Khanal, BofA: pressed on decision-making amid macro volatility; CEO Loeb replied, "The current one is no" regarding delays from geopolitical headlines and added, "I feel better about this year today than when we had our fourth quarter call."

  • Todd Thomas, KeyBanc: asked about data center supplier tailwinds; Loeb described demand as "mostly on the supply side" and Dunbar quantified, "of our 685,000 square feet of development leasing that we've done year-to-date, about half of that was related to data center-related type users."

  • Brendan Lynch, Barclays: asked how the Moody’s upgrade affects leverage flexibility; Tyler answered, "we have a lot of room, a lot of dry powder" and reiterated comfort in "sub-5x debt-to-EBITDA" over time.

  • John Kim, BMO: questioned implied occupancy decline in guidance; Tyler said, "we are projecting occupancy decline" based on "suite-by-suite" probabilities and added, "We typically run in that 75% customer retention."

  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho: asked where the company can be more opportunistic; CEO Loeb pointed to upside levers including "occupancy is a little bit better than we forecast" and said, "you'll probably see us... starting to think about" buying vacant buildings again as value-add opportunities.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical but constructive, focusing on the durability of faster leasing and the back-half ramp embedded in guidance; Goldfarb, Piper Sandler framed it as, "what's driving the sort of implied acceleration ramp in the back half?"

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A; Loeb used cautious language on durability, saying, "we're hopeful it's sustainable" and "I just hope it lasts," while Tyler repeatedly positioned guidance as a baseline with room to outperform.

  • Versus last quarter, management sounded more encouraged on near-term conditions; Loeb explicitly contrasted his view, saying, "I feel better about this year today than when we had our fourth quarter call," while still referencing "macro uncertainty" and headline risk.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted upward in multiple areas, with 2026 development starts increased to $265 million (from $250 million previously) and 2026 FFO midpoint raised to $9.52 per share (from the prior $9.40 to $9.60 range midpoint of $9.50).

  • The major narrative stayed consistent—development leasing taking longer—but the tone improved from Q4’s emphasis on “sustainability” to Q1’s emphasis on faster decisions in select cases; Dunbar said, "we are actually seeing some tenants move a little bit quicker than we have in the past."

  • Capital sourcing language evolved from monitoring equity as an option to action in Q1; Tyler said the $300 million proceeds assumption is now "a mix of debt and equity" after issuing $70 million via the ATM and adding $50 million of forward capacity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management repeatedly cited extended decision cycles and macro uncertainty; Dunbar said, "decision cycles continue to remain extended," while Tyler referenced "macro uncertainty" and "the tensions in the Middle East" as reasons not to push leasing assumptions.

  • Known vacancy pressure was disclosed in Tampa; COO Brent Wood said, "we do have 1 lease, a tenant in Tampa, 222,000 feet... that we know is going to vacate."

  • Sourcing constraints remain a strategic limiter; Dunbar said, "New development sites... remain challenging to source and entitlements and zoning continue to be difficult and time-consuming."

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q1 as a strong start driven by high occupancy, wide re-leasing spreads, and stronger development leasing momentum, while raising 2026 targets for FFO and development starts. The company described more flexibility on funding following equity issuance and highlighted balance sheet capacity after a Moody’s upgrade, but kept guidance built on conservative, suite-level leasing assumptions amid extended decision cycles, a known Tampa vacancy, and persistent entitlement friction.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Newmont in charts: Gold production declines 15% Y/Y
Morgan Stanley launches reserves portfolio for stablecoin issuers
data.description:
Morgan Stanley (MS) Investment Management launched the Stablecoin Reserves Portfolio, part of the Morgan Stanley Institutional Liquidity Funds trust, the company said Thursday.

The portfolio is a new government money market fund designed to align with the stablecoin reserves investment requirements of the Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins ((GENIUS)) Act. 

The fund offers payment stablecoin issuers an eligible money market fund option where they can invest their reserves that back their outstanding payment stablecoins.

"The significant increase in stablecoin issuers as well as the growing number of assets held in stablecoins represents an evolving portion of the marketplace that is ripe for future growth," said Fred McMullen, co-head of Global Liquidity at Morgan Stanley Investment Management.

The Stablecoin Reserves Portfolio seeks preservation of capital, daily liquidity, and maximum current income, consistent with seeking to maintain a stable $1.00 net asset value (NAV). The fund invests only in cash, U.S. Treasury bills, notes, and bonds with remaining maturities of 93 days or less, as well as certain overnight repurchase agreements collateralized by U.S. Treasury securities and/or cash.

Morgan Stanley (MS) stock slipped 0.5% in after-hours trading.

data.title: Southern Missouri Bancorp anticipates 19.5%-20% run-rate tax rate while targeting mid-single-digit loan growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Southern Missouri Bancorp (SMBC) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • President & Chief Administrative Officer Matthew Funke said results softened sequentially as “our earnings and profitability were down a bit from an increase in operating expenses and a modest uptick in provision for credit losses,” while also noting offsets from “a lower provision for income taxes, better noninterest income and slightly higher levels of net interest income.”

  • Funke emphasized profitability resilience despite seasonality, saying, “With maintaining an ROA above 1.40% the last 2 quarters, we feel good about what we've been able to achieve,” and reported “$1.60 diluted in the March quarter.”

  • Funke highlighted balance-sheet and capital actions, including gross loans up “$96 million during the third quarter,” a next-90-days pipeline “increased to $178 million,” and buybacks of “156,000 shares at an average price of $61.97 per share for a total of $9.7 million.”

  • Chairman & CEO Greg Steffens framed credit as stable but still elevated versus history, saying, “overall problem asset levels remain manageable,” while adding, “we're not complacent with current levels of problem assets” and expecting work-outs to continue moving forward.

  • Executive VP, CFO & Principal Financial Officer Stefan Chkautovich described funding-driven margin improvement and limits ahead: “This quarter's net interest margin of 3.67% was up 10 basis points compared to the linked December quarter,” but “we do not expect to see material near-term expansion of the NIM... without further rate cuts by the FOMC.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a mid-single-digit loan growth framework, with Funke stating that, after “achieving 5.4% loan growth in the fiscal year-to-date thus far, we're in a good position to reach the higher end of our anticipated mid-single-digit loan growth range for fiscal '26,” while flagging “some anticipated larger loan payoffs in the fourth quarter” that could mute near-term growth.

  • Chkautovich guided expectations for margin progression qualitatively, saying, “this coming quarter, our fourth quarter, I would expect sort of limited NIM expansion,” and added that deposit pricing tailwinds likely require further rate cuts: “I don't really see anything in the near term for a large incremental benefit without further rate cuts.”

  • On taxes, Chkautovich said, “we see our run rate effective tax rate to be in the range of 19.5% to 20%.”

Financial Results

  • Funke reported NIM of “3.67%” and said net interest income was “up just under 1% quarter-over-quarter and up just over 9% year-over-year,” attributing the year-over-year increase to “the increase in average earning asset balances and net interest margin expansion.”

  • Chkautovich detailed the cost-of-funds driver, stating the linked-quarter NIM improvement was “primarily driven by a 9 basis point improvement in our cost of funds to 2.52%,” while also noting “loan yields were flat quarter-over-quarter at 6.26%.”

  • Credit provisioning rose with growth and ag reserving: Chkautovich said the allowance for credit losses was “$55.9 million,” and “our provision for credit losses was $2.1 million in the quarter.”

  • Noninterest items and expense cadence were key swing factors: Chkautovich said noninterest income was up “$314,000 or 4.6%” sequentially, while “noninterest expense was up 3.8% quarter-over-quarter,” and later told analysts, “We think this quarter's run rate will be good to use for going forward.”

Q&A

  • Charles Driscoll, KBW: asked if deposit gathering can keep up given a loan-to-deposit ratio around 100%; President Funke: “Deposit growth is going to be a governing factor in how fast we can grow loans... The question is growing them at a low cost,” adding, “we still feel confident we can achieve that mid-single digit for the foreseeable future on both sides of the balance sheet.”

  • Driscoll, KBW: asked about buyback appetite; Funke: “it's probably a little higher than what we would like to see quarter-over-quarter,” and “if prices would improve from here, we'd expect activity to be a little bit more muted”; CEO Steffens: “the price determinant will determine how active we would be in stock repurchases.”

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: asked what’s driving pipeline strength; CEO Steffens: “we added several people 6 months ago, and we're seeing some of them hit their strides now,” and “we really haven't changed really much of any of our underwriting guidelines.”

  • Race, Piper Sandler: asked for fee income run-rate framing excluding discrete items; CFO Chkautovich: “the tax credit gain was about $305,000,” and said “nothing near term on the fee income side,” while noting focus areas “on wealth management, insurance and some other aspects.”

  • Race, Piper Sandler: asked about margin trajectory; CFO Chkautovich: “I would expect sort of limited NIM expansion,” citing maturing fixed-rate loans “repricing is a little bit lower,” and reiterated that large deposit-cost benefits likely need additional cuts.

  • Race, Piper Sandler: asked about timing of nonperformer resolutions and charge-offs; CEO Steffens: “we're really pretty optimistic that we'll start trending lower this quarter,” and “There could be some charge-offs related to one, but I don't anticipate it to have any impact on ACL or on our provision.”

  • Jordan Ghent, Stephens: asked for expense run rate; CFO Chkautovich: “We think this quarter's run rate will be good to use for going forward.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly cautious, centering on funding capacity (loan-to-deposit), sustainability of pipeline-driven growth, and normalization of fee income and expenses.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and mostly steady in Q&A, with confidence in profitability and credit work-outs (CEO Steffens: “we're pleased with the quarter and confident in our trajectory”) alongside guarded positioning on margin (CFO Chkautovich: “we do not expect to see material near-term expansion of the NIM... without further rate cuts”).

  • Versus last quarter, management commentary shifted from deposit-cost tailwinds and “positive trends continuing” to more explicit near-term NIM limits and stronger emphasis on deposit competition and wholesale funding mix.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Sequential profitability softened as Funke reported EPS of “$1.60” vs. “$1.62 per share diluted” last quarter, while margin expanded to “3.67%” from “3.57%.”

  • Balance-sheet growth accelerated, with Funke reporting loan growth of “$96 million” vs. “$35 million” last quarter, and deposits up “$33 million” vs. “$28 million,” alongside a clearer tilt back toward brokered funding this quarter.

  • Credit commentary was less centered on the prior quarter’s specialty CRE recovery narrative and more focused on managing elevated NPAs and ag-related reserve build; CEO Steffens emphasized continued workout progress and said the company is “not complacent.”

  • Capital deployment remained active, but repurchases were executed at a higher average price (“$61.97” vs. “$54.32”), and CEO Steffens added incremental balance-sheet flexibility with callable debt: “we have the capacity to retire $7.5 million of subordinated debt as it becomes callable in May.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Steffens flagged that “nonperforming assets, nonaccrual loans remain elevated compared to our historical levels,” and cautioned “we're not complacent with current levels of problem assets,” while pointing to workout execution as mitigation.

  • Agricultural pressure remained a key risk: Steffens said “‘26 is expected to be another challenging year, largely dependent on commodity prices,” and Chkautovich said reserves increased “largely by increased reserves on agricultural loans, reflecting ongoing pressure in the ag sector.”

  • Funding and margin risk was tied to deposit competition and rate policy: Funke said “local deposit rate competition was stiff,” while Chkautovich said meaningful NIM expansion is unlikely “without further rate cuts by the FOMC.”

  • CRE concentration was explicitly monitored, with Chkautovich stating nonowner-occupied CRE concentration was “approximately 291% of Tier 1 capital and allowance for credit losses.”

Final Takeaway

Management described a seasonally softer quarter with modestly higher provisioning and expenses, but maintained confidence in above-1.4% ROA trends, mid-single-digit loan growth objectives, and continued credit resolution. The company emphasized that near-term net interest margin expansion may be limited without further rate cuts, while capital flexibility is being used for buybacks, potential debt retirement, and an active M&A pipeline, alongside increased reserving for prolonged pressure in agricultural credits.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
